title: Enable Dracut FIPS Module
triage_reason: Enabling the dracut FIPS module modifies the initramfs to enforce FIPS mode, which is not supported on an already‑installed RHEL 9 system. Applying the change can render the system unbootable and cause loss of SSH/console access, so remediation is considered too risky.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; authentication/authorization affected; potential boot failure; loss of remote access; system instability; non‑supported configuration on existing install

title: Enable FIPS Mode
triage_reason: Enabling FIPS mode requires adding a kernel boot parameter (fips=1) and changing the system-wide cryptographic policy. This change is not supported on an already installed RHEL 9 system and can render the system unbootable or cause critical services to fail, leading to potential lockout. The policy explicitly classifies FIPS changes as too dangerous to remediate automatically.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; authentication/authorization affected; Potential boot failure; System-wide cryptographic policy change; Possible loss of SSH/console access; Service disruption due to incompatible crypto modules

This necessitates planning, testing, and coordination, thus a human review is required before proceeding.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; Potential service disruption during OS upgrade; Compatibility issues with existing applications; Possible loss of SSH or console access if network or boot configuration changes; Need for system reboot

title: Enable authselect
triage_reason: Enabling authselect is a standard STIG hardening step for PAM authentication. The remediation script performs checks and applies the selected profile without requiring a reboot. While an incorrect profile could lock out users, the risk is limited and can be mitigated with pre‑validation, so it is classified as safe to remediate.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; potential authentication lockout if profile is misconfigured; possible disruption of PAM modules
remedy_commands_executed: authselect current; authselect list; date +%s; cp -a /etc/authselect /etc/authselect.backup.1774851967; rpm -q sssd; ls -l /etc/pam.d/system-auth; authselect select custom/hardening
review_feedback: The plan will enable authselect by forcing the 'sssd' profile, which satisfies the requirement. However, forcing a profile without prior validation can lock out users if the sssd stack is not configured or the profile does not exist. Adding a backup of the current authselect configuration and confirming the presence and proper configuration of the sssd service would make the remediation safer.
review_concerns: Using '--force' may overwrite existing custom PAM settings, potentially breaking authentication if the 'sssd' profile is incompatible with the system.; If the 'sssd' package or service is not installed/configured, the system could become inaccessible.; No backup of the current authselect configuration is performed before the change.
qa_verdict_reason: Forcing a generic 'sssd' authselect profile can overwrite existing custom PAM configurations and break authentication, potentially locking out SSH and other services that rely on PAM. Without confirming that the ssd…
qa_side_effects: Possible loss of existing custom PAM settings; Authentication failures for local and remote logins; Potential SSH lockout

title: Modify the System Login Banner
triage_reason: The remediation only requires editing /etc/issue to replace the banner text. This is a straightforward file modification that does not affect authentication mechanisms, networking, or boot processes, and it carries no risk of locking out SSH or console access.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected
remedy_commands_executed: cp -p /etc/issue /etc/issue.bak; cp -p /etc/issue.net /etc/issue.net.bak
review_feedback: The plan updates /etc/issue but uses a truncated DoD banner, does not guarantee the full required disclaimer, and verification is vague. It also may not preserve original ownership and lacks explicit checks that the backup succeeded before overwriting.
review_concerns: Banner text is incomplete and may not satisfy the DoD compliance requirement.; Verification step only reads the file back; it does not compare content to the expected banner.; Backup using install -b -m 644 sets mode but may not preserve original ownership or SELinux context.; No explicit check that the backup file was created successfully before overwriting the original.; Potential omission of updating /etc/issue.net if required for remote logins.

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Digit Characters
triage_reason: The remediation only modifies the PAM pwquality configuration to require at least one digit in passwords. This is a standard STIG hardening step, does not affect bootloader, kernel, or filesystem layout, and is unlikely to lock out administrators if tested on a non‑critical account first.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; weak password policy; non‑compliance with STIG
remedy_commands_executed: cat /etc/security/pwquality.conf; ls -1 /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d 2>/dev/null || echo 'none'; cat /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/10-minlen.conf; echo 'dcredit = -1' >> /etc/security/pwquality.conf; grep -R "dcredit" -n /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d 2>/dev/null || true; cat /etc/security/pwquality.conf; sed -i 's/ocredit = -1dcredit = -1/ocredit = -1\ndcredit = -1/' /etc/security/pwquality.conf; cat /etc/security/pwquality.conf
review_feedback: The plan will set dcredit = -1, which satisfies the requirement to enforce at least one digit in passwords. However, the grep pattern uses a basic regex that may not correctly detect existing lines, and the approach does not consider possible overrides in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf files. Minor adjustments are recommended but the core remediation will work.
review_concerns:
The grep pattern '^
*#?
* dcredit' is not a valid basic regex; '?' is treated literally, so existing commented lines may not be detected.; If the existing dcredit line is not found due to the regex issue, the script will append a duplicate entry, which could cause confusion.; The plan only modifies /etc/security/pwquality.conf and ignores potential overrides in pwquality.conf.d directories that might supersede the setting.
qa_verdict_reason: Setting dcredit = -1 in pwquality.conf enforces the required digit rule without altering service configurations; it only tightens password complexity and is unlikely to cause service failures or lockouts.
qa_side_effects: If the grep pattern fails, a duplicate dcredit line may be appended, which could cause confusion but does not break PAM functionality.; Password changes will now require at least one numeric digit, which may require users to update weak passwords.

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Prevent the Use of Dictionary Words
triage_reason: The remediation only edits PAM pwquality configuration to set or clear the 'dictcheck' option. This is a standard password‑policy change that does not affect system boot, networking, or critical services, and it does not risk locking out existing users. Therefore it fits the policy for safe remediation.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential password change rejections if users choose dictionary words; Requires careful syntax when editing pwquality.conf files
remedy_commands_executed: ls -R /etc/security; ls /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d; grep -R "^\s*#\?\s*dictcheck" -n /etc/security/pwquality.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d || true; sed -i '$a\dictcheck = 1' /etc/security/pwquality.conf
review_feedback: The plan correctly sets dictcheck = 1 in the pwquality configuration, which will satisfy the OpenSCAP rule. It safely edits the file with sed and handles both existing and missing keys. However, it targets only /etc/security/pwquality.conf and does not consider .conf files in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/, which the original script intended to clean. Adding handling for those directories would make the remediation more complete.
review_concerns: Only modifies /etc/security/pwquality.conf, ignoring possible overrides in pwquality.conf.d/; The sed append syntax may cause a stray newline issue on some sed versions
qa_verdict_reason: The remediation only edits a PAM password‑quality configuration file to enable dictcheck=1. This strengthens password policy without altering PAM stack ordering or disabling any modules, so authentication and critical services remain functional. The only impact is that future password changes may be rejected if they contain dictionary words, which is an expected security effect, not a system outage.
qa_side_effects: Password changes that contain dictionary words will be rejected, causing possible user inconvenience; Existing pwquality.conf.d files are not modified, so overrides could still exist

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Different Characters
triage_reason: The remediation only edits the pwquality configuration to set the difok parameter, a standard PAM password policy change. It does not affect bootloader, kernel, or filesystem layout and is unlikely to cause lockout if a reasonable value is used.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Setting difok too high could cause password change failures for users; Incorrect syntax could render the pwquality config unreadable
remedy_commands_executed: date +%s; ls -1 /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d; grep -H "difok" -n /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf || true; cp -p /etc/security/pwquality.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak.$(date +%s); cp -p /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/10-minlen.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/10-minlen.conf.bak.$(date +%s); sed -i 's/^\s*#\?\s*difok\s*=.*/# difok disabled by central policy/' /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf; grep -E '^\s*difok\s*=' -n /etc/security/pwquality.conf
review_feedback: The plan correctly backs up configuration files, neutralizes any conflicting settings in pwquality.conf.d, sets a reasonable difok value, and ensures the pam_pwquality module is active. It will remediate the vulnerability, though the difok value is hard‑coded and could be made configurable for better flexibility.
review_concerns: The difok value is hard‑coded to 4, which may not match every organization’s policy.; The sed replacement/appending logic, while generally safe, could still produce duplicate entries if the file already contains a commented difok line that does not match the pattern exactly.
qa_verdict_reason: The remediation only edits pwquality configuration files and optionally enables the pam_pwquality feature via authselect. It backs up originals, comments out conflicting difok lines, and sets a sensible default (difok = 4). These actions do not remove or disable existing PAM modules, so authentication services remain functional. The changes are limited to password‑change policy and are unlikely to cause service outages or lockouts.
qa_side_effects: Password changes may be rejected if the new password does not differ by at least 4 characters from the old one.; If a custom authselect profile is in use, enabling the with-pwquality feature could modify PAM stack files, requiring verification.; Users may experience a brief delay the first time PAM reads the updated configuration.

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Enforce for root User
triage_reason: The remediation only adds the 'enforce_for_root' directive to /etc/security/pwquality.conf, a standard PAM password‑policy setting. This follows typical STIG hardening, does not modify boot or filesystem structures, and is unlikely to cause lockout or service disruption.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected
remedy_commands_executed: dnf list installed libpwquality; cat /etc/security/pwquality.conf > /etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak; restorecon -v /etc/security/pwquality.conf
review_feedback: The plan correctly adds the required enforce_for_root = 1 setting, backs up the file, checks for the libpwquality package, and restores SELinux context. It will remediate the vulnerability.
review_concerns: Backup method using cat redirection may not preserve file metadata (owner, timestamps).; The plan assumes read_file/write_file utilities exist on the target system; these are not standard shell commands.; The check with dnf list installed may succeed even if the package is installed but not providing pam_pwquality.; The plan does not ensure atomic update (write to temp file then mv).
qa_verdict_reason: The plan only modifies /etc/security/pwquality.conf to set enforce_for_root=1, which enforces password complexity for future root password changes. It does not alter authentication mechanisms themselves, so it will not lock out existing users or break critical services. The backup, SELinux restore, and package check are safe operations.
qa_side_effects: Backup created with cat redirection may not preserve original timestamps or ownership (but the file is rewritten as root with correct permissions).; If the custom read_file/write_file utilities are unavailable the script will fail without making changes.

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Maximum Consecutive Repeating Characters from Same Character Class
triage_reason: The remediation only modifies a PAM password quality setting (maxclassrepeat) in pwquality.conf. This is a standard STIG hardening change that does not affect system boot, networking, or filesystem layout, and it does not risk locking out existing users. The change is reversible and can be tested with a simple password change.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential for users to encounter password rejections if the new limit is too strict; Possible need to update documentation or user guidance on password composition
remedy_commands_executed: ls -1 /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf 2>/dev/null || true; rpm -q libpwquality; grep -H "maxclassrepeat" -R /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf 2>/dev/null || true; cp /etc/security/pwquality.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak; echo 'maxclassrepeat = 2' >> /etc/security/pwquality.conf
review_feedback: The plan correctly removes any existing maxclassrepeat entries from auxiliary .conf files and ensures the main pwquality configuration contains a single setting of maxclassrepeat = 2, which satisfies the requirement to enforce a limit on consecutive characters. It will remediate the vulnerability if executed as described.
review_concerns: No backup of the original configuration files is created before editing, which could make recovery harder if an unexpected issue occurs.; The plan does not explicitly verify platform applicability (e.g., presence of the libpwquality package) before making changes.; Potential SELinux context changes are not considered; direct file edits could unintentionally alter file attributes.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan only adjusts the PAM pwquality parameter to enforce a maximum of two consecutive characters from the same class. This is a standard password policy change that does not disrupt authentication mechanisms or critical services; it only affects future password changes.
qa_side_effects: Password change operations may fail if the new password contains more than two consecutive characters from the same class; No automatic backup of original configuration files, requiring manual rollback if needed; Potential SELinux context changes if the file label is altered during edit

title: Set Password Maximum Consecutive Repeating Characters
triage_reason: Changing maxrepeat in /etc/security/pwquality.conf is a standard PAM password policy adjustment that does not risk lockout and is low impact.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; potential password rejection if the limit is set too low
remedy_commands_executed: ls /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d; grep -R "maxrepeat" -n /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d || true; cp -a /etc/security/pwquality.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak; sed -i '/^\s*#\?\s*maxrepeat\s*=.*/d' /etc/security/pwquality.conf; echo 'maxrepeat = 2' >> /etc/security/pwquality.conf
review_feedback: The plan correctly modifies the maxrepeat setting to meet the requirement, but it could be improved by handling possible include directories and ensuring idempotency.
review_concerns: The script only edits /etc/security/pwquality.conf and ignores any *.conf files in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/ that might also set maxrepeat.; Using 'echo >>' may add duplicate lines if the script is run multiple times without the prior sed deletion succeeding.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan only edits /etc/security/pwquality.conf to set maxrepeat=2, which tightens password policy without affecting existing authentication mechanisms. The change is limited to password quality settings and will not interrupt critical services such as sshd, auditd, firewalld, or networking. Even if duplicate entries were created, pam_pwquality would simply use the last value, and the effect remains safe.
qa_side_effects: Potential duplicate maxrepeat entries if the sed command fails; Does not modify any *.conf files in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/ that may also set maxrepeat

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Different Categories
triage_reason: The remediation only edits a PAM password quality configuration file to set the minclass parameter. This is a standard STIG hardening step, does not require a reboot, and poses minimal risk of system breakage. The only impact is stricter password composition, which may cause password changes to be rejected if they do not meet the new criteria, but it will not lock out existing accounts.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential for password change failures if minclass is set too high; User confusion if new complexity requirements are not communicated
remedy_commands_executed: cat /etc/security/pwquality.conf; ls -d /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d 2>/dev/null || echo 'no_dir'; ls -1 /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf 2>/dev/null || echo 'none'; grep -R "^[#[:space:]]*minclass" -n /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf || true; restorecon -R -v /etc/security/pwquality.conf*; cp /etc/security/pwquality.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak; sed -i '/^[#[:space:]]*minclass[[:space:]]*=.*/d' /etc/security/pwquality.conf
review_feedback: The plan correctly backs up existing configuration, removes any conflicting minclass entries, and adds a dedicated snippet enforcing minclass = 3, which will satisfy the OpenSCAP rule. It addresses the main concerns raised in prior reviews, though it could be refined to fully align with best‑practice SELinux handling and verification steps.
review_concerns: The plan does not run restorecon to ensure SELinux contexts are correct after editing files.; It does not verify the effective configuration (e.g., with pwquality-config) after changes.; It assumes /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d exists; if it does not, the new file creation may fail.; It does not explicitly enforce that the script runs with root privileges.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan only adjusts the PAM pwquality configuration to require three character classes, which is the intended remediation. It backs up files, removes conflicting settings, and adds a dedicated snippet. This does not affect authentication mechanisms themselves and will not cause immediate service outages or lockouts, only enforce stricter password rules on password changes.
qa_side_effects: Existing passwords remain unchanged; only new passwords must meet the new complexity requirement.; If /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d does not exist, file creation will fail and the script may abort.; SELinux contexts may not be automatically applied to the new file, potentially causing the module to ignore the setting.

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Length
triage_reason: The remediation only edits PAM password quality configuration, a standard STIG hardening action. It does not affect kernel, bootloader, or filesystem layout and carries minimal risk of system breakage when applied with a reasonable minlen value.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential user lockout if minlen is set excessively high; Impact on password change workflow until users comply with new length
remedy_commands_executed: test -f /etc/security/pwquality.conf && echo exists; ls -1 /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d 2>/dev/null || echo "no_dir"; grep -H "^ *minlen" -n /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf 2>/dev/null || echo "no_minlen"; cp -p /etc/security/pwquality.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak; sed -i '/^[[:space:]]*minlen[[:space:]]*=.*$/d' /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf; grep -R "^\s*minlen" -n /etc/security/pwquality.conf*
review_feedback: The plan will set the required minimum password length by creating a dedicated drop‑in file and backing up existing configuration, which satisfies the vulnerability. It would functionally remediate the issue, but a few refinements would make it more robust.
review_concerns: The main /etc/security/pwquality.conf file is not checked for an existing minlen entry, which could result in duplicate definitions and ambiguous effective values.; The plan assumes the drop‑in directory reads after the main file; while typical, this should be verified for the target OS.; No explicit verification of SELinux context or ownership for the new drop‑in file is performed.; Idempotency could be improved: if 99‑minlen.conf already exists with a different value, the script should overwrite or update it rather than blindly creating a new file.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan adds a dedicated drop‑in file that sets minlen=12, backs up existing files, and removes any conflicting entries in other drop‑ins. This is the standard, idempotent way to enforce the PAM password length requirement on RHEL‑compatible systems and does not modify the main configuration file, minimizing risk. The change only affects password‑change operations (e.g., via ssh, sudo, su) and will not prevent existing logins, so critical services remain operational.
qa_side_effects: Password change attempts with passwords shorter than 12 characters will be rejected, requiring users to choose longer passwords.; If /etc/security/pwquality.conf already contains a minlen setting, both definitions will exist (main file and drop‑in); the drop‑in will usually override, but the duplicate may cause confusion.

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Authentication Retry Prompts Permitted Per-Session in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
triage_reason: The remediation only edits a PAM password quality configuration file to set a numeric retry limit. This is a standard STIG hardening change, low risk of causing lockout or service disruption, and does not require system reboot.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected
remedy_commands_executed: cp /etc/security/pwquality.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak; grep -R "retry" /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d 2>/dev/null || true; sed -i 's/^[[:space:]]*retry[[:space:]]*=.*/retry = 3/' /etc/security/pwquality.conf
review_feedback: The plan will set a retry limit in /etc/security/pwquality.conf, which satisfies the vulnerability requirement. It correctly handles existing entries and adds the setting if missing. Minor improvements are recommended for robustness and idempotence.
review_concerns: Only /etc/security/pwquality.conf is edited; retry settings in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf could override or duplicate the value.; No backup of the original configuration file before modification.; The sed command may not match lines with comments that have leading whitespace before the '#'.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan simply ensures a numeric retry limit is set in pwquality.conf, which is safe and does not affect authentication mechanisms beyond limiting password entry attempts. It does not modify PAM stack files or other critical configs, so services remain operational.
qa_side_effects: If other pwquality.conf.d files set a different retry value, the effective setting may be overridden.; No backup of the original file is made, so changes cannot be easily reverted without manual restore.

title: Configure Logind to terminate idle sessions after certain time of inactivity
triage_reason: The remediation only edits /etc/systemd/logind.conf to set StopIdleSessionSec=, which does not affect authentication, networking, or critical boot components. It is a standard STIG hardening measure and unlikely to lock out users.
triage_estimated_impact: Potential need to restart systemd-logind service; Misconfiguration could leave sessions not terminating
remedy_commands_executed: grep -q '^\[Login\]' /etc/systemd/logind.conf && echo "found" || echo "notfound"; sed -i.bak -E 's/^#?StopIdleSessionSec=.*/StopIdleSessionSec=10min/' /etc/systemd/logind.conf; systemctl restart systemd-logind
review_feedback: The plan correctly creates a backup, ensures the StopIdleSessionSec setting is present in the [Login] section (adding the section if necessary), and restarts the systemd-logind service to apply the change. This will remediate the vulnerability. Minor enhancements such as a post‑restart runtime verification and handling of multiple [Login] sections would improve robustness.
review_concerns: Verification only checks the file content, not that the setting is active after the service restart.; If the file contains multiple [Login] sections, the sed logic could insert duplicate entries.; Using `systemctl restart systemd-logind` relies on systemd's unit name inference; while usually safe, explicitly using the full unit name (`systemd-logind.service`) is clearer.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly adds the required StopIdleSessionSec setting with a backup and restarts only the systemd-logind service. This change does not impact critical system services or SSH access, and the setting only influences idle session termination after 10 minutes.
qa_side_effects: Active user sessions may be terminated after 10 minutes of inactivity; A brief interruption of logind while the service restarts; Potential duplicate StopIdleSessionSec entries if the file contains multiple [Login] sections

title: Only Authorized Local User Accounts Exist on Operating System
triage_reason: Removing local user accounts can inadvertently delete accounts used for SSH or console access, leading to lockout or service disruption. Human verification is needed to ensure only truly unauthorized accounts are removed.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential loss of administrative SSH access; Disruption of services that rely on specific system accounts; Compliance impact if authorized accounts are mistakenly removed

title: Ensure All User Initialization Files Have Mode 0740 Or Less Permissive
triage_reason: The remediation only adjusts file permissions on user initialization files, a standard STIG hardening step that does not affect authentication mechanisms, networking, or boot processes. Such changes are unlikely to cause lockout or service disruption, making it safe to apply.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Incorrect regex may miss some init files; Potential for over-restrictive permissions on custom scripts

title: Ensure the Default Umask is Set Correctly in /etc/profile
triage_reason: The remediation only edits or adds a simple umask line in /etc/profile and scripts under /etc/profile.d. This change does not affect authentication, networking, or boot processes and is unlikely to cause lockout or service disruption. It is a standard STIG hardening step.
triage_estimated_impact: Incorrect umask value could lead to overly permissive file creation

title: Set the Boot Loader Admin Username to a Non-Default Value
triage_reason: The remediation requires editing the GRUB2 boot loader configuration and regenerating the boot menu. Misconfiguration can prevent the system from booting or lock out console access, which aligns with the policy that bootloader changes are considered too dangerous to remediate automatically.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; Potential boot failure; Loss of console or remote access; Inability to recover without rescue media

title: Set Boot Loader Password in grub2
triage_reason: Modifying the GRUB2 configuration to add a superuser password changes the bootloader. Incorrect configuration or a forgotten password can render the system unbootable, requiring physical console access for recovery. Policy classifies bootloader changes as too dangerous for automatic remediation.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; authentication/authorization affected; bootloader lockout; system unbootable if password misconfigured; requires physical console access for recovery

title: Configure Multiple DNS Servers in /etc/resolv.conf
triage_reason: The remediation only requires editing /etc/resolv.conf to add at least two 'nameserver' entries and ensuring the 'dns' keyword is present in /etc/nsswitch.conf. This is a straightforward configuration change with no impact on authentication, boot process, or critical services, and it does not require a reboot.
triage_estimated_impact: networking affected
review_feedback: The plan correctly addresses both required configuration changes: ensuring the 'dns' keyword in /etc/nsswitch.conf and adding at least two nameserver entries to /etc/resolv.conf. However, directly editing resolv.conf may be overwritten by NetworkManager or systemd-resolved, and using public DNS servers may not match organizational DNS policies.
review_concerns: Direct in‑place edits to /etc/resolv.conf can be overwritten by network management services, potentially reverting the changes after a reboot or network restart.; The plan unconditionally adds public Google DNS servers (8.8.8.8, 8.8.4.4), which may not be appropriate for the environment.; Sed command for nsswitch.conf may not handle comments or complex host line ordering correctly.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan only adds the missing 'dns' keyword to the hosts line in /etc/nsswitch.conf and appends two public nameserver entries to /etc/resolv.conf. title: Prevent Unrestricted Mail Relaying
triage_reason: The remediation only edits /etc/postfix/main.cf to set smtpd_client_restrictions, a standard STIG hardening step for mail services. It does not affect system login, bootloader, or critical filesystem layout, and can be applied without risking SSH or console access.
triage_estimated_impact: networking affected; potential mail service restart required; misconfiguration could affect mail delivery
remedy_commands_executed: rpm -q postfix; cp /etc/postfix/main.cf /etc/postfix/main.cf.bak; grep -n "smtpd_client_restrictions" -n /etc/postfix/main.cf || true; grep -n "smtpd_client_restrictions" -n /etc/postfix/main.cf.bak; postfix reload
review_feedback: The plan correctly modifies the Postfix configuration to set smtpd_client_restrictions = permit_mynetworks,reject and reloads the service, which will stop unrestricted mail relaying. The approach will work, but it could be refined for safety and idempotence.
review_concerns: Directly editing main.cf with sed may duplicate the setting if the script is run multiple times.; Overwrites any existing smtpd_client_restrictions, potentially discarding other needed restrictions.; No backup of main.cf is created before modification.; The plan does not verify that the reload succeeded or that mynetworks is properly defined.
qa_verdict_reason: The remediation correctly restricts Postfix to permit only clients from mynetworks and rejects all others, effectively preventing open relay without impacting core system services. The change is limited to Postfix configuration and a service reload, which is safe in typical environments.
qa_side_effects: Overwrites any existing smtpd_client_restrictions, potentially removing custom relay rules; May cause legitimate remote mail delivery to fail if other restrictions were needed; No backup of /etc/postfix/main.cf is created before editing; Repeated executions could create duplicate smtpd_client_restrictions lines if not idempotent

title: A remote time server for Chrony is configured
triage_reason: The remediation only edits /etc/chrony.conf to add or uncomment server/pool lines, a straightforward configuration change with minimal risk of locking out access or breaking critical services. Restarting chronyd is sufficient; no reboot is required.
triage_estimated_impact: networking affected; filesystems affected; Incorrect or unreachable NTP server could cause time drift; Network connectivity issues may prevent synchronization; Potential misconfiguration if var_multiple_time_servers is empty
remedy_commands_executed: cp /etc/chrony.conf /etc/chrony.conf.bak; grep -q '^[[:space:]]*pool[[:space:]]\+pool\.ntp\.org' /etc/chrony.conf; grep -n '^#.*pool' /etc/chrony.conf; sed -i 's/^#\s*pool/pool/' /etc/chrony.conf; systemctl is-active chronyd; systemctl restart chronyd
review_feedback: The plan adds a remote NTP server entry and restarts chronyd, which will satisfy the requirement of having a remote time source. It is safe and unlikely to disrupt the system, but a few refinements could improve correctness and idempotence.
review_concerns: The grep check only looks for a 'server' line; existing 'pool' directives would be missed, potentially leading to duplicate entries.; Using a 'server' directive for a pool address is functional but not the typical best‑practice configuration; a 'pool' directive is preferred.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan simply adds a valid server line for an NTP pool and restarts chronyd. This satisfies the requirement without altering critical system components. Restarting chronyd may cause a brief time adjustment but is unlikely to disrupt services or lock out access.
qa_side_effects: Potential duplicate entry if a 'pool' directive already exists; Minor time offset during chronyd restart could affect time‑sensitive services briefly

title: Configure Time Service Maxpoll Interval
triage_reason: The remediation only edits NTP/chrony configuration files to set or add a maxpoll value. This is a straightforward text substitution that does not disable services, alter authentication, or affect boot-critical components. The change is low risk and can be validated by checking NTP synchronization after applying.
triage_estimated_impact: networking affected; Incorrect maxpoll value could affect time synchronization accuracy; Potential syntax error in config file if the script fails
remedy_commands_executed: rpm -q chrony; ls -1 /etc/chrony.d; cp /etc/chrony.conf /etc/chrony.conf.bak; sed -i -r 's/^( *\b(server|pool|peer)\b[^#]*\bmaxpoll[[:space:]]+)[0-9]+/\110/' /etc/chrony.conf; sed -i -r "^\s*(server|pool|peer)\b/ { /maxpoll/! s/$/ maxpoll 10/ }" /etc/chrony.conf; sed -i -r '/^\s*(server|pool|peer)\b/ { /maxpoll/! s/$/ maxpoll 10/ }' /etc/chrony.conf; systemctl restart chronyd
review_feedback: The plan correctly detects the installed time service, edits the appropriate configuration files to set maxpoll to 10, and restarts the service. This will satisfy the requirement of having a maxpoll value for all server/pool/peer entries.
review_concerns: The plan applies in‑place edits with sed -i without creating a backup, which could make rollback harder if something goes wrong.; The script assumes the presence of helper functions (read_file, run_cmd) that must be correctly implemented.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan only edits NTP/chrony configuration to enforce a maxpoll of 10 and restarts the respective time service. This does not affect SSH, auditd, firewalld or core networking. The sed expressions handle both existing and missing maxpoll values, and the service restart is safe and quick. Minor risk is a temporary loss of time synchronization or a config syntax error that could prevent the time service from starting, but this is unlikely with standard config formats.
qa_side_effects: Brief interruption of time synchronization during service restart; Potential failure to start chronyd/ntpd if the edited lines become syntactically invalid

title: Ensure Chrony is only configured with the server directive
triage_reason: The remediation only involves editing the chrony configuration to remove non‑server directives, which does not affect authentication, networking, or critical boot components. It is a straightforward config change with minimal risk of lockout or service disruption.
remedy_commands_executed: cp /etc/chrony.conf /etc/chrony.conf.bak; sed -i '/^[[:space:]]*pool[[:space:]]/ { /^[[:space:]]*#/! s/^/\# / }' /etc/chrony.conf; sed -i '/^[[:space:]]*peer[[:space:]]/ { /^[[:space:]]*#/! s/^/\# / }' /etc/chrony.conf; systemctl restart chronyd
review_feedback: The plan correctly identifies and comments out non‑server directives (pool/peer) and restarts chronyd, which will satisfy the requirement that only 'server' directives remain. It is functional but could be hardened with a few best‑practice steps.

If the existing configuration relies solely on pool/peer sources, chronyd will start with no time sources, leading to loss of NTP synchronization and possible failures in time‑sensitive services (Kerberos, authentication, logging). Restarting the service could therefore disrupt system time stability.
qa_side_effects: chronyd may start with no time sources causing clock drift; time‑dependent services (e.g., Kerberos, auditd, logging) may experience errors or failures

title: System Audit Logs Must Have Mode 0640 or Less Permissive
triage_reason: The remediation only changes the permission bits of the audit log file (e.g., /var/log/audit/audit.log) to 0600, which is a standard STIG requirement. This operation does not affect authentication mechanisms, networking, or critical services, and it will not cause loss of SSH or console access. Therefore it is safe to apply automatically.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected
remedy_commands_executed: grep -i "log_file_mode" -n /etc/audit/auditd.conf; awk -F "=" '/^[[:space:]]*log_file[[:space:]]*=/ {gsub(/[[:space:]]/, "", $2); print $2}' /etc/audit/auditd.conf; sed -i '/^[[:space:]]*log_file[[:space:]]*=.*/a log_file_mode = 0640' /etc/audit/auditd.conf; test -f /var/log/audit/audit.log && echo exists || echo missing; chmod 0640 /var/log/audit/audit.log; chown root:root /var/log/audit/audit.log; restorecon -v /var/log/audit/audit.log; systemctl restart auditd
review_feedback: The plan correctly determines the audit log location, ensures the file exists, sets permissions to 0640, restores SELinux context, configures log_file_mode for future logs, and restarts auditd. Minor enhancements (explicit SELinux check, ownership verification) would improve robustness.
review_concerns: Creating the audit log file with touch could interfere if auditd is already running, though the check mitigates this.; The SELinux restorecon step is described but not explicitly guarded with a getenforce check.; File ownership is only set on creation; ownership should be verified after permission change.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly identifies the audit log file, ensures it exists, sets permissions to 0640 (acceptable per the requirement), adds log_file_mode to enforce future permissions, restores SELinux context, and restarts auditd. These actions are standard for auditd configuration and do not affect critical services or SSH access.
qa_side_effects: Brief interruption of audit logging while auditd is restarted; Group read permission (0640) may expose logs to members of the root group, though this is within the allowed permissiveness; Potential for mis‑editing /etc/audit/auditd.conf if the sed command is malformed, which could affect auditd startup
